whyOneLine: every C++ program is just CPU instructions running in order.
code is text humans read and the compiler turns into machine action.
your work lives in .cpp files; this is the input the compiler reads.
compiler turns text into a runnable .exe before any user sees output.
running the .exe is what produces the output the SIT102 tester checks.
terminal output is where every Q1 trace and Q4 print lands.
terminal input is what cin reads from in Q3 read functions.
without #include <iostream> the compiler has never heard of cin or cout.
demoCode: #include <iostream>

whyOneLine: lets you write cout instead of std::cout; SIT102 always uses this.
every program starts at main(); Q4 demands you write it from scratch.
demoCode: int main() { return 0; }

whyOneLine: missing one ; is the most common SIT102 compile fail.
demoCode:
int x = 5;        // ; required
cout << x;        // ; required

whyOneLine: { marks where every function body, loop body, and struct body begins.
} closes the matching { ; mismatched braces collapse the whole parse.
main must end with return 0; or the OS treats the program as failed.
// lets you annotate code without affecting behavior; useful in answers.
demoCode:
int x = 5;     // declare counter
x = x + 1;     // increment

whyOneLine: the compiler ignores extra spaces and blank lines; readability is for you.
indentation makes structure visible; the compiler does not require it.
primary tool for showing program results; chained in every Q4 print loop.
demoCode: cout << "hello";

whyOneLine: << sends a value to cout; chain it to print several things in one line.
demoCode:
int x = 5;
cout << x;

whyOneLine: string literal in quotes goes directly to the terminal.
demoCode: cout << "hello, world";

whyOneLine: an int written to cout becomes its decimal digits on screen.
demoCode:
cout << 5;
cout << 42;

whyOneLine: endl writes a newline and flushes; what every Q1 trace expects between lines.
demoCode:
cout << "line one" << endl;
cout << "line two" << endl;

whyOneLine: "\\n" is the shorter newline used in Q4 printf format strings.
demoCode:
cout << "line one\n";
cout << "line two\n";

whyOneLine: chain << << << to put several values on one line of output.
demoCode:
int a = 1, b = 2;
cout << a << " " << b << endl;

whyOneLine: Q4 mocks the SIT102 printf style for formatted record printing.
demoCode:
int id = 42;
printf("id = %d\n", id);

whyOneLine: %d in printf is where each int (id, count) lands in the output.
demoCode:
int n = 7;
printf("count = %d\n", n);

whyOneLine: %s in printf is where each C-string (use .c_str()) lands.
demoCode:
string name = "ada";
printf("name = %s\n", name.c_str());

whyOneLine: \\n inside a printf format adds a newline at that exact spot.
demoCode:
printf("hello\n");
printf("world\n");

whyOneLine: first arg is the format string; the rest fill the % placeholders in order.
demoCode:
printf("score = %d\n", 100);
//      ^^^^^^^^^^^^^ format string

whyOneLine: extra args after the format must match the placeholders one-for-one.
a variable is a labeled box in memory; everything else builds on this.
demoCode:
int n = 5;        // n is a labeled box holding 5
n = n + 1;        // box now holds 6

whyOneLine: every variable has a name you use to read and write its box.
every variable has a type that decides what fits in the box.
every variable in C++ starts with a typed declaration.
demoCode:
int x;            // declare an int box named x
string title;     // declare a string box named title

whyOneLine: type name = value; declares and initializes in one statement.
use the variable's name to read its current value into an expression.
demoCode:
int x = 5;
cout << x;         // reads x's value

whyOneLine: name = newValue; overwrites the box with a new value.
demoCode:
int x = 5;
x = 10;            // overwrites the box

whyOneLine: = writes a value; comparing equality is == (a frequent Q1 trap).
C++ reads top-down; declare a variable before you can use it.
demoCode:
int x = 5;        // declared first
cout << x;        // then used

whyOneLine: int is the default type for counts, indices, and Q4 record IDs.
demoCode:
int count = 7;
int n = 42;

whyOneLine: double is what stores the .mystery field in Q1 stat_double.
demoCode:
double mystery = 0.0;
double price = 3.14;

whyOneLine: string holds names and Q4 record fields like book.title.
demoCode:
string title = "C++";
string author = "ada";

whyOneLine: bool is the type that an if condition expects.
demoCode:
bool ok = true;
bool done = false;

whyOneLine: char holds one letter; rare in T2 but part of the type system.
demoCode:
char letter = 'A';
char digit = '7';

whyOneLine: literal integers like 5 fix the type as int unless cast.
demoCode:
int n = 5;          // 5 is an int literal
int big = 1000000;

whyOneLine: decimals like 3.14 default to double; appears in Q1 .mystery seeds.
demoCode:
double pi = 3.14;
double half = 0.5;

whyOneLine: string literals in double quotes need <string>; appear in Q2 fields.
demoCode:
string greeting = "hello";
cout << "literal text";

whyOneLine: single-quoted 'a' is a char, not a string; mixing the two is a Q4 bug.
demoCode:
char letter = 'A';
char comma = ',';

whyOneLine: true and false are the only two bool literals; used in Q1 conditions.
demoCode:
bool ok = true;
bool failed = false;

whyOneLine: without #include <string> the string type is not declared.
Q3 read function calls cin once per struct field; without it no user data.
demoCode:
int n;
cin >> n;          // pause for user input

whyOneLine: >> reads the next whitespace-separated token into the variable.
demoCode:
int x;
cin >> x;          // >> reads next token

whyOneLine: cin >> x; pauses until the user types and presses enter.
demoCode: cin >> x;

whyOneLine: tabs, spaces, and newlines all separate input tokens for >>.
demoCode:
int a, b;
cin >> a >> b;     // user types: 3 7

whyOneLine: cin reads ints as digits, strings as one whitespace-delimited word.
chain >> >> to read several values from one input line.
demoCode:
int a, b, c;
cin >> a >> b >> c;

whyOneLine: getline reads an entire line into a string; rare but useful for Q3 names.
demoCode:
string fullName;
getline(cin, fullName);

whyOneLine: + adds two numbers; appears in Q1 mystery-update lines like data.a + data.b.
demoCode:
int sum = 3 + 4;        // 7
int total = a + b;

whyOneLine: - subtracts; sometimes appears in Q1 traces under stat_double mutations.
demoCode: int diff = 10 - 3;      // 7

whyOneLine: * multiplies; appears in Q1 area-style mocks and some mystery formulas.
demoCode: int area = width * height;

whyOneLine: / divides; for ints this truncates toward zero (a known Q1 trap).
demoCode: int half = n / 2;

whyOneLine: % gives the remainder of int division; useful for parity tricks.
demoCode: int rem = 7 % 3;        // 1

whyOneLine: 5/2 in int arithmetic is 2, not 2.5; affects who_am_i mystery values.
*, /, % bind tighter than + and -; matters in mixed expressions.
() force the desired evaluation order regardless of precedence.
x += 1 is shorthand for x = x + 1; matches mystery-update style.
demoCode:
int total = 0;
total += 5;             // total = total + 5

whyOneLine: i++ adds 1 and is what every for-loop step in Q3 and Q4 uses.
i-- subtracts 1; rarely on T2 but completes the operator family.
== tests if two values are equal; conditional trap when written as =.
!= is the opposite of ==; used in some Q1 mystery conditions.
i < n is the canonical loop guard for Q3 and Q4 for-loops.
data.numbers[i] > data.mystery is a Q1 max-finder shape.
<= includes the boundary; off-by-one risk on Q3 read loops.
>= appears in some Q1 conditional variants.
every comparison returns a bool, which is what if and while expect.
demoCode:
bool match = (x == 5);   // bool result
if (match) cout << "y";

whyOneLine: && chains two bool tests; both must be true for the whole to be true.
|| means OR; either condition true makes the whole true.
! flips a bool; rare in T2 but completes the logical family.
if (cond) runs its block when the condition is true.
else runs the alternative block when the if condition is false.
else if chains decisions; only the first matching branch runs.
the if condition must reduce to a bool; raw ints implicitly convert.
always wrap if bodies in {} ; SIT102 grading rejects single-line shortcuts.
while (cond) repeats while the condition stays true; check first.
do { body } while runs the body once before the first cond check.
for is what every Q3 read loop and Q4 print loop is built from.
the init clause runs ONCE before the loop starts.
the condition is checked BEFORE each iteration body runs.
the step clause runs AFTER each iteration body completes.
break leaves the loop immediately; rare in T2 but valid.
continue skips the rest of the body and goes to the next iteration.
nested loops are how you would walk a 2D grid; rare in T2.
if the condition is never false, your code hangs at runtime.
a function is a named block of code you can reuse and call from main.
the signature shape: returnType name(params) { body } -- write it cold.
name(args); is how the caller actually runs the function.
demoCode:
int sum = add(3, 4);
cout << sum;       // 7

whyOneLine: each parameter becomes a local box inside the function frame.
return value; ends the function and reports a result to the caller.
void means the function does work but does not hand back a value.
you must declare or prototype a function before any caller uses it.
prototype lets you put main first and define helpers later.
demoCode:
int square(int);      // prototype: types only
void greet(string);

whyOneLine: locals (and params without &) vanish at return; their changes are lost.
main cannot poke at a function's locals; only what & or return exposes.
C++ default: every parameter is a copy; the caller box is untouched.
without &, a function's mutation lives only inside the frame and dies.
& binds the parameter to the caller's box; same memory, two names.
& sits between the type and the parameter name in the function signature.
with &, every mutation persists; the caller sees the change after return.
you cannot bind a reference to a literal; needs a real variable.
const & lets the function read the caller box without ever writing it.
the same memory box wears two names while the function runs.
array stores N values of one type back-to-back in memory.
demoCode: // array holds N items in a row

whyOneLine: type arr[N]; reserves space for N elements at compile time.
demoCode: int arr[5];

whyOneLine: type arr[N] = { ... }; fills the slots in order.
arr[0] is the first slot; off-by-one bugs start when you forget this.
demoCode: arr[0]   // first slot

whyOneLine: arr[i] is how Q3 and Q4 read or write the i-th record.
demoCode: arr[i]

whyOneLine: C++ arrays count from 0; arr[N] is past the end.
the last valid index is N-1, the canonical loop upper bound minus one.
demoCode: arr[N-1] // last slot

whyOneLine: writing past arr[N-1] is undefined behavior; the SIT102 grader catches it.
you set N at declaration; fixed forever for that array.
demoCode: const int MAX = 100; int arr[MAX];

whyOneLine: C++ has no `return arr;`; you must pass it via & in the parameter list.
demoCode:
// C++ has no `return arr;`. Use a reference parameter instead:
void fill(int arr[], int n) { /* ... */ }

whyOneLine: for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) is the canonical full-array walk.
demoCode: for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) arr[i] = ...;

whyOneLine: arr[i] = v stores v at slot i; what Q3 read and Q4 print depend on.
type arr[] in a parameter is the array-passing shape -- without &, copies.
demoCode: void f(int arr[], int n) { ... }

whyOneLine: &arr[] aliases the caller's array; what makes Q3 reads stick.
demoCode: void f(int &arr[], int n) { arr[0] = 99; }

whyOneLine: struct lets you carry several related fields under one variable name.
demoCode: // struct = a named bundle of named fields

whyOneLine: struct Name { fields }; defines the type before any code can use it.
each field has its own type and name; comma-separated lists are illegal.
the ; after } is mandatory; missing it is a textbook SIT102 fail.
demoCode: struct Point { int x; int y; };

whyOneLine: after the struct exists, Name x; declares one of its instances.
demoCode:
struct Point { int x; int y; };
Point p;

whyOneLine: x.field is how you read or write one of the struct's slots.
demoCode:
Point p;
p.x = 3;

whyOneLine: x.field = v; updates that one field and leaves the others alone.
demoCode:
Point p;
p.x = 3;

whyOneLine: cout << x.field prints the field's current value to the terminal.
demoCode:
Point p; p.x = 3;
cout << p.x;

whyOneLine: a struct can hold an array as a field; this is the Q1 stat_double shape.
data.numbers[i] is the Q1 chained access pattern -- struct then array.
demoCode:
stat_double d;
d.numbers[0] = 1.0;

whyOneLine: Name list[N]; is the Q3 and Q4 array-of-structs pattern.
demoCode:
struct Point { int x; int y; };
Point list[3];

whyOneLine: list[i].field is the Q3/Q4 chained access -- pick a record, then a field.
demoCode:
Point list[3];
list[0].x = 5;

whyOneLine: passing a struct by value copies every field; the caller is untouched.
demoCode: void f(Point p) { p.x = 99; }   // caller's p unchanged

whyOneLine: void f(Name &x) lets the function mutate the caller's struct directly.
demoCode: void update(Point &p) { p.x = 99; }

whyOneLine: with &, x.field = v inside the function persists into the caller.
void f(Name &list[], int n) is the Q3 read function signature shape.
demoCode: void read_items(Item &list[], int count) { ... }

whyOneLine: list[i].field = v writes into the caller's array of structs via &.
const Name & gives the function read access without write power.
demoCode: void print(const Point &p) { cout << p.x; }

whyOneLine: Q1 demands you simulate a function's execution by hand and predict d.mystery.
demoCode: // No code - meta-skill atom

whyOneLine: track every variable's value step by step; one missed update sinks the answer.
every assignment overwrites the old value; record the new state.
for-loop traces show i changing from start through cond exit.
an if test picks ONE branch; ignore the unused side completely.
calling a function opens a new frame with fresh local boxes.
return collapses the frame; locals without & cease to exist.
if the parameter has &, your traced edits write through to the caller box.
arr[i] = v writes through to the caller array if the array param is &.
trace simple assignments: end with the final value of every box you wrote.
for arrays, write the final state of every slot you touched.
loop traces walk every iteration until the condition fails.
branch traces commit to one side based on the live condition value.
function-call traces step into the new frame and back out at return.
mark the SAME-box rule for & params; that single line decides Q1.
Q1 is a max-finder algorithm: scan all numbers, keep the biggest.
& on the struct param means d.mystery you set inside is what main sees.
data.numbers[i] > data.mystery is the comparison every Q1 mystery rests on.
Q2 wants a struct definition with three fields, written from a spec.
each field is one declaration: type fieldName; on its own line.
demoCode:
int id;
string description;

whyOneLine: always end the closing } with a semicolon; this is graded literally.
pick types per the spec: int for ids, string for names, double for amounts.
field order does not affect the grade as long as all three are present.
Q3 always returns void -- the function fills caller storage, not a value.
demoCode: void read_items(...) { ... }

whyOneLine: Type &list[] is the array-by-ref parameter shape that makes writes stick.
demoCode: void read_X(X &list[], int count) {}

whyOneLine: int count tells the function how many records to read.
demoCode: void read_X(X &list[], int count) {}

whyOneLine: for (int i = 0; i < count; i++) is the loop frame Q3 expects.
demoCode: for (int i = 0; i < count; i++) { ... }

whyOneLine: cin >> list[i].field; reads one user input into one field of one record.
demoCode: cin >> list[i].id;

whyOneLine: one cin line per struct field -- the function reads them all in order.
an optional cout prompt before each cin makes the program user-friendly.
demoCode: cout << "Enter id: "; cin >> list[i].id;

whyOneLine: Q4 expects a complete int main() that returns 0 at the end.
const int MAX = 100; defines the array capacity before any other code.
demoCode: const int MAX = 100;

whyOneLine: Type list[MAX]; reserves storage for up to MAX records.
demoCode: computer_data list[MAX];

whyOneLine: int count; declares the variable that will hold the actual record count.
demoCode:
int count;
cin >> count;

whyOneLine: cin >> count; reads the user's record count into the variable.
demoCode: cin >> count;

whyOneLine: call read_X(list, count); to fill the array via Q3's function.
demoCode: read_book(list, count);

whyOneLine: for (int i = 0; i < count; i++) loops over the records you just read.
demoCode: for (int i = 0; i < count; i++) printf(...);

whyOneLine: printf with format string + .c_str() prints each record on one line.
demoCode: printf("%d %s\n", list[i].id, list[i].title.c_str());

whyOneLine: C++ string -> %s needs .c_str() ; without it printf prints garbage.
demoCode:
string name = "ada";
printf("%s\n", name.c_str());   // C-string for %s

whyOneLine: first full timed mock; covers all four questions end-to-end.
second full mock; entity name varies but the four shapes hold.
third mock; tightens timing under the 90-minute budget.
fourth mock; adds tricky stat_double mystery formulas to Q1.
final mock; production-readiness check before exam day.
